pr_review_body: LGTM Thx @krassowski

The comments in the source code look very relevant and useful but let's pretend just for argument's sake that the person reading the code has no time or not enough background to dive deep into a 8 years old python bug or CPython source code. Any ressemblance with any real person is of course a pure coincidence.

The pointers seem extremely useful for a reader wondering whether this sort of black magic is the correct _solution_ or not. But long before that, it would be good to have a very basic introduction to the _problems_ solved by this code. This could be done by answering some very basic questions like "Why catch `KeyboardInterrupt` at all; why doesn't Python automagically does the right thing? Does it in a later/future Python? Is this mimicking newer Python versions to support older Python versions" and "Why is some `signal` propagation required on Linux but not on Windows?"

You get the idea.

LGTM. A couple minor comments.

> We will select all of asset b's downstreams as well as asset b itself because the function returns:

I think this would make `include_self` not useful for the scenarios that I had imagined using it in, e.g. "launch a run to materialize all the assets in set X when all the assets upstream them have been materialized", where X might include assets that depend on other assets in X.

Could we make it work the other way? I.e. have `AssetSelection(x).upstream(include_self=False)` resolve to something like `AssetSelection(x).upstream().resolve(...).difference(AssetSelection(x).resolve(...))`?

That makes me think that we could actually offer `.difference` and/or the `-` operator on `AssetSelection`s.  E.g. support `AssetSelection(x).upstream() - AssetSelection(x)`. But I don't have a strong opinion on whether this is important to do or better than `include_self`.

Overall the direction makes sense, a little confusing though so I just wanted to clarify.

Previously we had three types of task
(1) Actor creation with dynamic options
(2) Actor creation
(3) Task
And two types of worker:
(A) dedicated worker
(B) worker

(1) used its own map (A), while (2) and (3) drew from the list (B).

- Prior to this PR, we could actually reuse a (3) worker for (2).  But in the current PR, we disallow this. Is this intended / is my understanding correct?
- Prior to this PR, we had a map for (A), which allowed (1) to look up the specific worker it needed. (The worker process needed to have been started with the correct command line options `dynamic_options`). But in the current PR, we got rid of (A) and don't seem to check for the specific worker anywhere (I would expect this logic to appear near where we scan through the list of workers and "skip if the runtime env doesn't match".)  So it seems like (1) could pop an arbitrary worker that didn't have the correct dynamic options. Is this an issue?

I'm pretty sure that `impl Display for Key` _should_ actually display the Python `repr` for the `Key`.

If there are particular codepaths (such as the one for `Snapshot`, or other workunit rendering code) that don't want to display the "entire" underlying object, I think that they should use the `EngineAware` trait to conditionally render `Key`'s/`Value`s based on their types.

A middle ground would be to have `impl Display for Key` use the `EngineAware` trait under the hood... I'm less sure that that is a good behavior though, because it feels like the `EngineAware` trait should be specific to workunit creation.

Regarding naming, I take back my suggestion to create a *metadata* subpackage. It's unlikely that we will create a common traditional metadata class for tuf and in-toto (#272), which such a package would have been a good fit for. Because tuf and in-toto already have working metadata implementations, which they actually want to move away from in favor of DSSE.

So I suggest the following renames for this PR:
- util.py -> _internal/utils.py 
- metadata.py -> dsse.py
- test_metadata.py -> test_dsse.py

Did some extensive testing!

This weekend I was playing with a profiler, to find out why this lib is so slow.
More on that in different PR's.

I've tested this code.. 
The readability is a question of personal preference. (I like a bit of splitting in several functions :+1: )
The negative thing of the splitting is performance.
The number of function calls is exploding with this code.
(as each loaded template will have multiple calls)
And function calls are quite expensive in python performance wise.
Resulting in the code being 1,5x times as slow in the profiler.

Testing on a single invoice.
old code:  2579 function calls
this pr: 293413 function calls

So my vote is to not merge this pr in the current state.
Rather use it as an inspiration.
- Update variable names
- format logger strings
- tests for the loader.

FYI:
I've also made an intermediate version, of this code with the old code.
(Less splitting of functions) But still I came to the conclusion that performance is greatly impacted by this. So better not to split the loader functions.

Hmm, so I thought you had a good argument here for keeping smoothing out of the line chart: https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/pull/4359#issuecomment-730706272

Do we not have the same reasons to keep outlier pruning out of the line chart?  I guess perhaps it's slightly different since it doesn't change the actual data, but it also seems like something that's logically a little separate.  In the post above you made the point that PR curves don't need smoothing; they also don't need to ignore outliers (and they do need to set the Y-range to a pre-determined value, so if we expose that option, the ignore-outliers mode could just be code at the callsite that generates the extent to use?).

Also, the motivation of making it easier to change the outlier logic seems like a strong one, since I've always disliked how we interpret "ignore outliers" as just truncating 10% of the data no matter what the distribution is; I would be much happier if we could do something that's more sensitive to the actual distribution, like excluding points beyond X% of the inter-quartile range, or beyond 2 standard deviations, etc.  At that level it might even make sense as a tunable parameter like smoothing.

Hey thanks for the PR! I know the rest of my review is quite negative, but I genuinely appreciate the time and energy you've put into putting this together!

I don't like this change because it provides the wrong feedback to a not an insignificant group of users. If I (as a Python 3 only user) give Black an invalid file, the Python 3.X parse attempts will fail and then Black exits with a "Black attempted to parse python2 code, [...] please install typed_ast" error... which makes no sense to me as a Python 3 only user. Not only is this hiding the true problem: the input file isn't valid Python 3 code, the confused user, might try the advice to install `typed_ast` which 1) might not work or be a pain, and 2) doesn't actually the fix the issue.

Personally I'm open to pushing most if not all of the disruption to the users still using Black's Python 2 support. My idea would be that Black would need `--target-version py27` (configured automatically via magic or configured manually) to even attempt Python 2 parsing. Although this falls apart when Black is formatting multiple files at once (since someone is bound to have a mix of Python 2 and 3 files... of which our target-version logic can't really describe).

There's not much actionable in these two paragraphs of feedback. It's something to just think about, perhaps someone else (maybe you!) has a better idea!

Quick high-level comment: IIUC, this will include the time between an RPC request being sent and receiving an RPC response as the queue time for the handler; given that the global mean/max queue times are used to get a sense of event loop contention, including this out-of-loop RPC time in the event loop queue time stats might confuse this metric.

This is one of the reasons that I've been suggesting that we should keep the RPC stats separate from the event loop stats, since they're fundamentally measuring different things (what's on the event loop, whether there's contention vs. what RPCs are in flight, how long they take).

Thanks. I think we probably want to make this more intelligent. Rather than always creating a target at the BUILD root, create it for each source tree. 

So, if your repo only has `prod/terraform/`, create at `prod/terraform/BUILD`. If you have `service1/terraform/` and `service2/terraform`, then create at `service1/terraform/BUILD` and `service2/terraform/BUILD`. Concretely, create a `terraform_modules` target in the most ancestor directory that has a `.tf` file.

The main benefit imo is locality, that metadata should be defined close to what it is describing.

If we're going to be adding support for different certificate lifetimes, we're probably also going to need to do something about `renew_before_expiry`, or renewal won't work as expected. It currently defaults to `30 days` and can only be overridden by manually modifying the renewal parameters, after the fact.

Potentially:

- If not otherwise specified, we can modify it to automatically calculate a suitable duration based on the lifetime of the certificate we received. Let's Encrypt's advice is "We recommend renewing certificates automatically when they have a third of their total lifetime left", but that needs to be validated for different lifetimes I think.
- We might want to add `--renew-before-expiry` as a CLI flag.

@avnishn Thanks a ton man. It's great that you made a working version of multi-gpu trainer. But this PR needs a good amount of work and re-design iteration until it's ready for production and aligned with the original design. I left some comments here and there for the immediate things that came to my mind. But more fundamentally, I think you need to take a step back and re-think about the structure of the abstractions and class hierarchies. Basically think about which parts are framework specific, which parts are not, How would a user know what to implement from the base class to be able to extend it and so on. You need to put these design decisions into a design doc so that we can discuss alternatives and whether we are leaking unwanted information to other places of the code-base.  Another question that you should be asking is how we make this code as simple as possible, and very easy to understand and follow. The principle is simple, you need to shard the data, run parallel fwd passes, run parallel backward passes and aggregate the result. Don't think that documentation will solve this problem, you need a better and simpler design with no information leakage between modules. Writing a documentation for this kind of code-base would be very easy.

I would agree that add a test using portabledevice to the project, under the following conditions ...

- Assume the target device is a sandbox consisting of a few folders and a few very small files.
- Use `os.path`, `glob`, `pathlib`, etc. to check if the target device has the same directory structure as the expected sandbox or skip the test if not.
- Eliminate the using conditional branches such as inside of `findDir` that are not used to skip or fail tests.
- Make assertions that inspect contents of the target device.
- If doing something like `RemoteWrite`, create a temporary folder using `tempfile` or something similar and write to it.
- If possible, assert after the test that the sandbox is not different from before the test.

Existing this test is no matter, however, be that as it may, I think it is necessary to test `_fix_inout_args` in another environment-independent way, since it is difficult to always require such a sandbox in developer and CI environments.

I'm not a huge fan of defining these TypedDicts in `if TYPE_CHECKING` block, but it is currently indeed necessary as they're 3.8+, and mcstatus still supports 3.7. 

However doing this then leads to using type ignores when actually passing these dicts (although we could also use casts like `cast("RawResponsePlayer", {name="foo", id="bar"})`, which I would personally prefer). Considering the goal of this PR is to avoid type needing casts, this isn't ideal.

The way we would normally do this is to create instances of typed dicts directly, like: `RawResponsePlayer(name="foo", id="bar")`, which gives us `dict` objects on runtime, but obviously this is impossible considering we can't use these classes on runtime.

But there is a way to get around this nicely, and still essentially allow creation of these typed dicts on runtime, avoiding the need for casting (or type-ignores) completely. To do this, we can use logic like this:
```py
if TYPE_CHECKING:
    class Foo(TypedDict):
        ...
else:
    Foo = dict
    
x = Foo(...)
assert type(x) is dict  # x is simply a dict on runtime (same as initializing a typed-dict in 3.8)
typing_extensions.reveal_type(x)  # x is of type Foo (TypedDict) - evaluated only at typing-time
```

That said, I also wouldn't necessarily be completely against simply dropping 3.7 entirely, as most distributions don't ship it anymore (certainly not the bigger ones) and it is fairly outdated by now (it was released in 2018, and is already in only security fix phase, meaning no more bugs will be fixed and only major security issues will be addressed. The final end-of-life for 3.7 is still relatively far off though - 2023-06, more in [PEP 537](https://peps.python.org/pep-0537/)). But obviously, this would require another PR.
